   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "# Placebo Method\n",
    "\n",
    "The first method is called the Placebo method and is related to the permutation framework of inference. \n",
    "\n",
    "The basic idea is that we start a loop around all of the control units. These are units where no intervention happened. In each iteration of the loop, we treat a control unit as if it were the treated unit i.e. create a synthetic control and predict a counterfactual.\n",
    "\n",
    "In the graph below, we can see this method in action. The gray lines are \"plaebo units\" i.e. units that had no intervention, but we treated as if they were. We can then see the estimated effect for each of these units. We can compare this against the actual treated unit/s. If the difference in post-treatment effects between the placebo and treated units are large, we interpret this as evidence that the intervention did have an effect. \n",
    "\n",
    "Not only that, this procedure assumes a random assignment of the intervention, which is hard to believe for this kind of policy intervention (see Abadie, 2021)"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 14,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [
    {
     "data": {
      "image/png": "",
      "text/plain": [
       "<Figure size 640x480 with 1 Axes>"
      ]

    }

   "source": [
    "p_value, rj, dfr = placebo(pds, SyntheticControl, plot_1=True, plot_2=False, penalty='l2')"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "In this method, you may notice that some of the placebo units have poor pre-treated fit. There are a couple ways to handle this. First, we can heuristically trim the placebos units to only include matches that have a similar pre-treatment fit as the treated unit. \n",
    "\n",
    "\n",
    "\n"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 15,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "# Implement that here"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Another approach is to take the ratio of pre-treatment MSE and post-treatment MSE to create an index illustrating how much of an effect the treatment had on the treated and placebo units. For example, if a unit had a good pre and post treatment fit the ratio will be close to one, inversely if a unit had a poor pre and post treatement fit the ratio will also be close to one. When the ratio grows larger it means an and effect is being observed. We can then compare the ratios of placebo units and treated units and calculate a p-value. "
   ]
  },
  {

   "execution_count": 16,

   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "\n",
    "# Conformal Inference\n",
    "\n",
    "Loop through the treated units. \n",
    "For each unit:\n",
    "    Loop through the treated time periods. \n",
    "        For each time period loop through a set of null hypothesis. \n",
    "\n",
    "While Conformal Inference is proven to be robust in many scenarios, it is not guaranteed to work everytime. Most commonly, Conformal Inference can fail when\n",
    "\n",
    "there is not enough days in the Pre-Treatment Period\n",
    "the true impact (called in the paper as Shock Sequence Ut) is not stationary or not weakly dependent\n",
    "the data is not stationary and not weakly dependent \n",
    "when our model is misspecified or inconsistent"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Not exactly the same as **reference** this is because of the optimizer -- just an example of the sensitivity of some of the methods. \n",
    "\n",
    "Below, we run with the optimizer from the example. "
   ]

   "source": [
    "import matplotlib.pyplot as plt\n",
    "\n",
    "def plot_many_dist(dists):\n",
    "    fig, ax = plt.subplots()  # Create a figure and axes object\n",
    "    for dist in dists:\n",
    "        lower_90, mean, upper_90 = dist\n",
    "        std_dev = (upper_90 - lower_90) / (2 * 1.645)  # 1.645 is the z-score for 90% confidence interval\n",
    "        x = np.linspace(lower_90 - 3*std_dev, upper_90 + 3*std_dev, 1000)\n",
    "        y = (1/(std_dev * np.sqrt(2 * np.pi))) * np.exp(-0.5 * ((x - mean) / std_dev)**2)\n",
    "        ax.plot(x, y, label=get_variable_name(dist))  # Use the axes object to plot on the same figure\n",
    "        \n",
    "    ax.set_xlabel('Value')\n",
    "    ax.set_ylabel('Probability Density')\n",
    "    ax.set_title('Distribution Plot with 90% Prediction Interval')\n",
    "    ax.legend()  # Add a legend to differentiate the distributions\n",
    "\n",
    "    fig.show()"
   ]
  },

    "def get_variable_name(var):\n",
    "    frame = inspect.currentframe().f_back\n",
    "    variables = frame.f_locals.items()\n",
    "    return [name for name, value in variables if value is var]\n",

    "    frame = inspect.currentframe().f_back\n",

   ]

   "source": [
    "import matplotlib.pyplot as plt\n",
    "import numpy as np\n",
    "import inspect\n",
    "\n",
    "def get_variable_name(var, local_vars):\n",
    "    return [name for name, value in local_vars.items() if value is var]\n",
    "\n",
    "def plot_many_dist(dists):\n",
    "    fig, ax = plt.subplots()  # Create a figure and axes object\n",
    "    frame = inspect.currentframe().f_back\n",
    "    local_vars = frame.f_locals\n",
    "\n",
    "    for dist in dists:\n",
    "        lower_90, mean, upper_90 = dist\n",
    "        std_dev = (upper_90 - lower_90) / (2 * 1.645)  # 1.645 is the z-score for 90% confidence interval\n",
    "        x = np.linspace(lower_90 - 3*std_dev, upper_90 + 3*std_dev, 1000)\n",
    "        y = (1/(std_dev * np.sqrt(2 * np.pi))) * np.exp(-0.5 * ((x - mean) / std_dev)**2)\n",
    "        variable_name = get_variable_name(dist, local_vars)\n",
    "        ax.plot(x, y, label=variable_name[0] if variable_name else 'Unknown')  # Use the axes object to plot on the same figure\n",
    "\n",
    "    ax.set_xlabel('Value')\n",
    "    ax.set_ylabel('Probability Density')\n",
    "    ax.set_title('Distribution Plot with 90% Prediction Interval')\n",
    "    ax.legend()  # Add a legend to differentiate the distributions\n",
    "\n",
    "    plt.show()\n"
   ]
  },
